The article "Enhancing Smart IoT Malware Detection: A GhostNet-based Hybrid Approach" proposes a novel six-step framework to detect and categorize IoT malware. The framework uses a combination of techniques, including Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA), feature engineering, and ensemble learning.

The approach starts by using eight malware attack datasets as input, followed by insights from EDA. Feature engineering involves scaling, One-Hot Encoding, target variable analysis, feature importance using MDI and XGBoost, and clustering with K-Means and PCA.

The GhostNet ensemble, combined with the Gated Recurrent Unit Ensembler (GNGRUE), is trained on these datasets and fine-tuned using the Jaya Algorithm (JA) to identify and categorize malware. The tuned GNGRUE-JA is then tested on malware datasets.

The study compares the proposed model's performance with existing methods, evaluating metrics such as AUC, accuracy, recall, and hamming loss. The results show that the proposed model outperforms existing methods by around 15% across these metrics, with a 10% reduction in time complexity.

Overall, the study's outcomes emphasize the importance of achieving cost-effective solutions for detecting and categorizing IoT malware, particularly in the context of smart cities and advanced manufacturing.
